steps.title: Link share to possible growth
steps.content: The study compared each brand's market share with how much purchasing one product raised the probability of purchasing the same brand in another category. An inverse relationship means higher-share brands showed smaller increases, while lower-share brands showed larger increases.

steps.title: Rule out unrelated mechanisms
steps.content: Choice A focuses on how often a product category is bought, not on cross-category lift. Choice B discusses competition within one category. Choice D would weaken the effect for low-share brands because consumers would not recognize the shared brand.

steps.title: Use the ceiling effect
steps.content: High-share brands are already likely to be bought, so purchasing the paired product leaves less room for their purchase probability to rise. Low-share brands have more room for a cross-category boost. The answer is C.
